framework: A path qualifies only when >=3 prominent, proven people in the field visibly share the capability, each with a resolving link — the pattern across standouts is the evidence, not market demand. Ranked by strength of that pattern. Curated editorial, not DOL-derived.

paths.path: Author IETF RFCs that become deployed internet infrastructure
paths.what_it_is: Writing and shepherding an Internet-Draft through IETF working-group review until it is published as an RFC that vendors and operators actually implement, not just proposing an idea informally.
paths.why_it_signals: Randy Bush, Job Snijders, and Radia Perlman each turned protocol work (BGP/RPKI security, spanning tree/TRILL) into formally ratified, widely-deployed IETF standards, and employers can verify the exact standard a candidate shipped rather than take their word for design skill.

paths.path: Maintain an open-source routing daemon or protocol tool relied on in production
paths.what_it_is: Becoming a committer or maintainer on a widely-deployed open-source networking project (a BGP/routing daemon, LLDP implementation, or protocol tooling) rather than only operating vendor black boxes.
paths.why_it_signals: Vincent Bernat (lldpd), Donatas Abraitis (FRRouting), and Martin Mareš (BIRD Internet Routing Daemon) all built reputations network operators trust specifically because their code runs inside real production routing stacks used by ISPs, IXPs, and hyperscalers.

paths.path: Publish public technical writing that teaches network design at scale
paths.what_it_is: Regularly writing blog posts, books, or measurement reports that explain how large networks actually behave and are designed, aimed at other engineers rather than marketing.
paths.why_it_signals: Ivan Pepelnjak, Russ White, and Geoff Huston each built decades-long public bodies of writing (ipSpace.net, rule11.tech, potaroo.net) that turned their operational network-design judgment into a durable, citable public record other architects rely on.
